pages.title: Property Division in Ontario
pages.metaTitle: Property Division in Ontario | Equalization & Net Family Property
pages.metaDescription: A comprehensive Ontario guide to property division for married spouses: equalization of net family property, excluded property, and matrimonial home rules.

contentBlocks.subhead: A detailed guide to equalization, Net Family Property, exclusions, and the unique matrimonial home rules.

contentBlocks.heading: Overview
contentBlocks.html: Property division is often the most financially significant part of an Ontario separation. For married spouses in Ontario, property division is generally done through equalization of Net Family Property (NFP), which compares each spouse's increase in net worth during the marriage.

contentBlocks.title: Key takeaway
contentBlocks.html: Ontario does not usually divide each asset item-by-item. Instead, it equalizes the growth in net worth during the marriage.

contentBlocks.heading: Who This Applies To
contentBlocks.html:
Married spouses in Ontario typically use the equalization regime under the Family Law Act.

Common-law partners are generally not entitled to equalization by default.

contentBlocks.label: Build your Ontario agreement with Our Separation

contentBlocks.heading: Core Definitions
contentBlocks.html:
Valuation Date

The valuation date is typically the date the spouses separated.

Net Family Property

Each spouse's NFP is their net worth on the valuation date minus their net worth on the date of marriage.

contentBlocks.heading: How Equalization Works
contentBlocks.html:
The spouse with the higher NFP pays the other spouse one-half of the difference.

Formula: (Higher NFP − Lower NFP) ÷ 2

contentBlocks.heading: The Matrimonial Home
contentBlocks.html: The matrimonial home receives special treatment. If a property is the matrimonial home on the valuation date, Ontario does not allow the owning spouse to deduct the home's value on the date of marriage.

contentBlocks.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
contentBlocks.html:
Do we have to sell our house?

Not necessarily. One spouse can buy out the other's share.

What about gifts and inheritances?

Gifts and inheritances may be excluded if they can be traced properly.

How are pensions valued?

Pensions require professional valuation.
